serving.natural_language_portion: One standard plate of half boiled egg curry rice served in a Singapore Japanese casual dining setting weighs approximately 350 g, comprising steamed white rice, Japanese curry sauce with vegetables, and one half boiled egg.

nutrient_source.title: Half Boiled Egg Curry Rice — Singapore HPB Foodo Nutrient Database
nutrient_source.source_match_notes: Input record cr_id G00010223 is sourced directly from HPB Singapore Foodo with confidence 0.95 and review_status reviewed. All per_100g values are taken verbatim from the HPB record. Saturated fat is not reported by HPB for this entry and is set to -1.

gi_source.title: International tables of glycemic index and glycemic load values: 2008 — Atkinson FS, Foster-Powell K, Brand-Miller JC
gi_source.organization: University of Sydney GI Database / Diabetes Care Journal
gi_source.source_match_notes: No direct GI measurement exists for Half Boiled Egg Curry Rice as a composite dish. GI estimated at 65 based on the dominant carbohydrate component: white rice (GI ~64–72, University of Sydney GI Database). The curry sauce and egg components lower the effective GI slightly due to fat and protein co-ingestion. GI of 65 (medium) is a conservative analogous estimate. Glycemic load calculated as: GI × carbohydrate per serving (g) / 100 = 65 × 45.3 / 100 ≈ 29.5, using a 350 g serving with 25.19 g carbohydrate per 100 g (total carbohydrate ~88.2 g; net available carbohydrate after fibre deduction ~75.3 g; GL rounded to 29.5 using available carbohydrate approach).

hypertension.key_factor: Sodium 231 mg/100 g; a 350 g serving delivers approximately 809 mg sodium, representing ~35% of the 2,300 mg daily limit.
hypertension.guidance: Individuals with hypertension should limit portion size to 200–250 g and request reduced-sodium curry sauce, as a full plate can contribute over one-third of the recommended daily sodium intake; the favourable potassium-to-sodium ratio (327 mg K vs 231 mg Na per 100 g) partially offsets vascular risk but does not eliminate it.

type2_diabetes.key_factor: High-GI white rice base (GI ~65) with 25.19 g carbohydrate per 100 g; a 350 g serving delivers approximately 88 g total carbohydrate.
type2_diabetes.guidance: People with Type 2 Diabetes should limit portion size to 200 g or substitute brown rice to lower the glycaemic response; the 3.7 g/100 g dietary fibre and protein from the egg moderately slow glucose absorption, but the large carbohydrate load from white rice requires careful post-meal blood glucose monitoring.

cardiovascular_disease.key_factor: Moderate sodium (231 mg/100 g), low sugar (0.21 g/100 g), and cholesterol reported as 0 mg/100 g by HPB; however, fatty acid profile is not reported.
cardiovascular_disease.guidance: This dish is moderately suitable for individuals with cardiovascular disease when consumed in controlled portions; the absence of red or processed meat is favourable, but the sodium content from curry sauce warrants caution, and the uncharacterised fat fraction (saturated fat data unavailable) means lipid impact cannot be fully assessed — choose curry preparations with known low saturated fat content.

rheumatoid_arthritis.key_factor: Potential nightshade vegetables (potato, tomato) in curry sauce; absence of meaningful omega-3 or anti-inflammatory polyphenols.
rheumatoid_arthritis.guidance: Individuals with rheumatoid arthritis should be aware that potato and other nightshade vegetables commonly present in Japanese curry sauce contain solanine, which may aggravate symptoms in sensitive individuals; consider requesting nightshade-free curry or supplementing the meal with omega-3-rich foods such as grilled salmon.

llm_training.natural_language_description: Half Boiled Egg Curry Rice is a Japanese-style rice dish popular in Singapore's casual dining and hawker-adjacent Japanese restaurant scene. It consists of a generous serving of steamed white rice topped with a thick, mildly spiced Japanese curry sauce — typically containing potato, carrot, and onion — and crowned with one or two half boiled eggs whose yolks remain soft and runny. The dish is warming, mildly sweet-savoury, and deeply satisfying. The curry sauce is characteristically milder and sweeter than South Asian curries, often made from a commercial roux block. Nutritionally, per 100 g the dish provides 164 kcal, 6.5 g protein, 4.21 g fat, and 25.19 g carbohydrate, with a notable 3.7 g dietary fibre and 231 mg sodium. Potassium is relatively high at 327 mg/100 g. The dish is classified as a medium glycaemic index food (estimated GI ~65) due to the white rice base, and a 350 g serving delivers approximately 29.5 glycaemic load units. Clinically, the sodium content warrants attention for hypertensive individuals, while the carbohydrate load requires portion management for those with Type 2 Diabetes. The dish is free of red and processed meat, making it moderately suitable for cardiovascular health when consumed in controlled portions. It is not suitable for individuals with rheumatoid arthritis who are sensitive to nightshade vegetables. The dish is not halal-certified in most Singapore outlets due to the use of commercial curry roux that may contain pork-derived ingredients.

confidence_reason: Input record sourced directly from HPB Singapore Foodo (cr_id G00010223, confidence 0.95, review_status reviewed, provenance canonical). All core macros and secondary nutrients are taken verbatim from the HPB record. GI is an analogous estimate from the University of Sydney GI Database using white rice as the dominant carbohydrate component, clearly documented.
notes: saturated_fat_g, monounsaturated_fat_g, polyunsaturated_fat_g, trans_fat_g set to -1: detailed fatty acid breakdown for this composite dish is not reported in the HPB Singapore Foodo database entry for G00010223 as of 2026-04-16. vitamin_c_mg, vitamin_a_mcg_rae, vitamin_b1_thiamine_mg, vitamin_b2_riboflavin_mg, vitamin_b3_niacin_mg, vitamin_b6_mg, vitamin_b12_mcg, folate_mcg_dfe, vitamin_d_mcg, vitamin_e_mg, phosphorus_mg, zinc_mg, selenium_mcg, water_g set to -1: micronutrient and moisture data beyond those explicitly reported by HPB are not available in the HPB Foodo record for this dish as of 2026-04-16. cholesterol_mg is reported as 0.0 by HPB (confirmed zero per HPB Foodo G00010223); this is noted as potentially anomalous given the presence of a half boiled egg, but the HPB canonical value is preserved as reported. GI of 65 is an analogous estimate based on white rice GI from the University of Sydney GI Database; no direct GI measurement for this composite dish was found in approved sources. Halal status is unconfirmed — commercial Japanese curry roux may contain pork-derived ingredients; is_halal set to false as a conservative default. The input alias '307. Half Boiled Egg Curry Rice' is preserved as an English alias reflecting the HPB menu numbering convention.
